metadata.title: The First Two Minutes: Title + Problem (Slides 1–2)
metadata.playlist: Sell the Vision — Your Investor Pitch Video
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 brief — Video Pitch to Investors: Slides 1–2 (title + problem)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 2 of 7. The first two minutes of your pitch are the only two that are guaranteed to be heard. Investors make decisions about whether to keep listening in the first minute. If the problem is not clear by minute two, nothing that follows will land the way it needs to. This episode covers slides 1 and 2: open memorable and make them feel the pain. Slide 1 is your project name, your name, and a one-line description that makes a non-technical person immediately understand what this tool is and who it is for. Slide 2 is the problem — what is broken, who suffers from it, and how much it actually costs them in time, money, or frustration. Both slides together take 90 seconds. Both need to work for someone who has never heard of your project.

props.segment: The First Two Minutes: Title + Problem (Slides 1–2)
props.command: Turn my Conductor Brief problem statement into a quantified, non-technical problem slide — with a one-liner for slide 1 and a numbered pain statement for slide 2.
props.runningText: the first two minutes are the only two that are guaranteed to be heard — open memorable, then make them feel the pain…
output: Slide 1: project name · your name · one-line description (no jargon, 15 seconds)
Slide 2: the problem quantified in dollars, time, or frustration (labeled numbers)
Both slides: land for a non-technical person who has never heard of your project

beats.narration_text: Slide 1 is the title slide. It has three things: your project name, your name, and a one-line description. That is it. The one-liner is the hardest part. It should answer two questions in one sentence: what does it do, and who is it for? This is the same sentence you wrote in Conductor Brief Part 1 and refined in Assignment 6 as your UVP. If you have a strong Part 1 sentence, use it here — you have already done this work. What makes a one-liner strong: it names a specific person, a specific outcome, and does not use any technical terms the audience would need explained. Weak: 'An AI-powered multi-agent workflow platform for marketing automation.' Strong: 'An AI assistant that writes your weekly performance report before your Monday standup.' The investor reads the strong version and immediately knows: who it is for, what problem it solves, and what the world looks like after the tool exists. That is 15 seconds. A strong one-liner earns attention for the next 7 minutes and 45 seconds.

props.artifactHeading: Project name · your name · one memorable line
artifactLines: Three things only: project name · your name · one-line description. 15 seconds.
The one-liner = Part 1 sentence / A6 UVP. You already wrote this. Use it.
Weak: 'AI-powered multi-agent workflow platform for marketing automation.' (jargon, no person)
Strong: 'An AI assistant that writes your weekly performance report before Monday standup.'
Test: names a specific person · a specific outcome · zero unexplained jargon.
props.sparkLine: Strong one-liner earns the next 7 minutes and 45 seconds.

beats.narration_text: Slide 2 is the problem slide. Its job is to make the investor feel the pain before you offer the solution. The structure is three parts: what the problem is, who specifically suffers from it, and how much it actually costs them. The 'who' is the same specific person you named in Conductor Brief Part 2A — your user profile. Do not say 'businesses' or 'teams.' Say 'email marketing coordinators at agencies with 10 to 50 clients.' The 'how much' is a number — in time, money, or frustration — and it should be labeled as an estimate if you do not have a primary source. 'Email marketing coordinators at digital agencies spend an average of 3 hours per day compiling performance reports manually — time that could be redirected to strategy.' That is a problem slide. It names the person, names the cost, and implies the value of the solution without stating it. The investor test: could you build a back-of-envelope ROI argument from this slide alone? If the answer is yes, the problem slide is doing its job.

props.artifactHeading: What's broken · who suffers · how much it costs
artifactLines: 3 parts: what the problem is · who specifically suffers (from Part 2A) · cost in time/money/frustration.
Not 'businesses' or 'teams.' Specific: 'email marketing coordinators at agencies with 10–50 clients.'
Quantify: 'spend an average of 3 hours/day compiling reports manually.' Label rough numbers as estimates.
Example: 'Email coordinators spend 3 hrs/day on manual reporting — time that could go to strategy.'
Investor test: can they build a back-of-envelope ROI argument from this slide alone? If yes: pass.
props.sparkLine: Name the person. Name the cost. Let the ROI imply itself.

beats.act: why quantification works — the ROI signal
beats.narration_text: Why does quantification matter? Because investors think in multiples. If you tell them 'people waste time on reporting,' they have no way to evaluate the size of the opportunity. If you tell them 'email coordinators at agencies spend 3 hours per day on manual reporting — and there are approximately 200,000 marketing coordinators in the US earning an average of $55,000 per year — you are describing a labor cost of roughly $18 billion that a tool like yours could address,' now they have something to think about. You do not have to do the full market-size calculation on the problem slide. You just have to put a number on the pain. The number does the work. It tells the investor: this person's time is worth something, there are many of them, and a solution has a real addressable value. Label your numbers. 'Estimate based on BLS Occupational data' or 'rough estimate' is fine. Invented precision is not fine — and a skeptical investor will challenge a number that sounds too round. One real labeled number is stronger than three impressive-sounding uncited statistics.

props.artifactHeading: Numbers give investors something to think about
artifactLines: 'People waste time' → no size, no opportunity. A number transforms it.
'3 hrs/day × 200k coordinators × $55k/yr ≈ $18B labor cost' → now there's a market.
You don't need the full TAM on slide 2 — just put a number on the pain.
Label your sources: 'BLS estimate' or 'rough estimate' is fine. Invented precision is not.
One real labeled number > three impressive-sounding uncited statistics.
props.sparkLine: Label your numbers. One real number beats three uncited ones.

beats.narration_text: The four traps that kill slides 1 and 2 before the investor reaches slide 3. First: a forgettable title. If your one-liner has jargon, a category descriptor instead of a benefit, or is too long to say in one breath, rewrite it. The test is: read it aloud to someone outside tech. Do they understand immediately what the tool does? Second: a vague problem. 'Teams aren't as efficient as they could be' is a mood, not a problem. A problem has a person, an action, and a cost. Third: an unquantified problem. If you describe the problem without a number, you are asking the investor to estimate the opportunity size themselves — which they will not do in your favor. Add a number and label it. Fourth: no named person. 'Companies' and 'organizations' are not users. The email marketing coordinator is a user. Name the role. The investor should be able to picture the specific person who is suffering before slide 2 ends.

artifactLines: Trap 1: forgettable one-liner. Jargon or category descriptor = not memorable. Read it aloud to someone outside tech.
Trap 2: vague problem. 'Teams aren't efficient' is a mood — a problem needs a person, an action, and a cost.
Trap 3: no number on the pain. Unquantified pain asks the investor to estimate the opportunity — they won't.
Trap 4: no named person. 'Companies' and 'organizations' are not users. Name the role.
Test: by end of slide 2, can the investor picture the specific suffering person? If not: rewrite.
props.sparkLine: A problem has a person, an action, and a cost. Not a mood.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Episode 2. Slide 1: your project name, your name, and one line that names a specific person and a specific outcome — no jargon, 15 seconds. If you wrote a strong Part 1 sentence for the Conductor Brief, use it. Slide 2: the problem, quantified. Name the person who suffers — from your Part 2A user profile. Name the cost in time, money, or frustration. Label any rough estimates. The investor test for slide 2: can they build a back-of-envelope ROI argument from that slide alone? Traps: vague one-liner, mood instead of problem, no number on the pain, no named person. By the end of minute two, the investor should know exactly who suffers, how much it costs them, and feel the weight of it before you offer the solution.

props.artifactTitle: The First Two Minutes: Title + Problem
artifactLines: Slide 1: name · project · one-liner (specific person, specific outcome, zero jargon). 15 seconds.
Slide 2: named person (Part 2A) · quantified cost · labeled numbers. 75 seconds.
Investor test: can they build an ROI argument from slide 2 alone?
Trap: mood instead of problem ('teams aren't efficient'). Add a person, an action, a cost.
Trap: no number. One labeled estimate > three uncited statistics.
By minute 2: the investor feels the pain before you offer the solution.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude. Fill in the brackets before running. The prompt reads: Here is my Conductor Brief Part 1 sentence: [paste it]. Here is my Part 2A user profile: [paste it]. Write Slide 1 and Slide 2 of my investor pitch. For Slide 1: give me the project name, my name placeholder, and three variations of the one-line description — each naming a specific person and a specific outcome, with no technical jargon. For Slide 2: write the problem in plain English. Include the specific user from Part 2A. Quantify the pain in time, money, or frustration — label rough numbers as estimates. Make the problem specific enough that an investor could build a back-of-envelope ROI argument from this slide alone. After the prompt runs: say slide 1's one-liner aloud to someone outside tech. Do they immediately understand what the tool does? If they need to ask a follow-up question, rewrite it. For slide 2, does it name a specific person? Does it have a number? Is the number labeled? Fix any of these before moving to Episode 3.

props.segment: The First Two Minutes: Title + Problem (Slides 1–2)
props.command: Here is my Part 1 sentence: [paste it]. Here is my Part 2A user: [paste it]. Write Slides 1–2. Slide 1: project name, name placeholder, 3 one-liner variations (specific person + outcome, no jargon). Slide 2: plain-English problem, named Part 2A user, quantified pain (time/money/frustration), labeled estimates. Make it specific enough an investor can build an ROI argument from slide 2 alone.
props.runningText: paste your Part 1 sentence and Part 2A user before running — the problem slide only lands if it names the right person…

beats.narration_text: The First Two Minutes: Title + Problem. Liam, in for Bear.
